response:
The error you encounter stems from a version mismatch where your WPF project targets .NET Framework 4.0 while your main application runs on .NET 7. This discrepancy causes the runtime to search for legacy assemblies that do not exist in the modern .NET environment, resulting in the file not found exception.

To resolve this without downgrading your main application, you must update your WPF library project to target .NET 7 or a compatible .NET version that supports cross-platform builds. This ensures that the WPF components are compiled with the correct runtime expectations for the modern framework.

cousin projects in your solution must be configured to use the same target framework as the main application to prevent assembly loading failures. By aligning the target frameworks, you eliminate the need for the system to load the outdated PresentationFramework version that triggered the initial error.

You should also verify that your project files explicitly reference the correct NuGet packages for WPF on .NET 7, such as Microsoft.Windows.Compatibility if you need specific legacy APIs. This step guarantees that all necessary dependencies are resolved at compile time rather than failing at runtime.

Finally, clean and rebuild your entire solution after making these changes to ensure no cached binaries from the previous configuration remain. This fresh build process will correctly link the WPF UI components to the .NET 7 runtime, allowing your cross-platform app to function on both Windows and Mac.
